attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a three-hour history of sudden onset abdominal pain, mainly in the right upper quadrant and periumbilical region. The pain is described as sharp. He has a medical history of a myocardial infarction five years ago. His medications include aspirin and atenolol. He has a 20 pack-year smoking history.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 94/min, blood pressure 142/86 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. His body mass index is 34 kg/m2, which makes the abdominal examination difficult, but nothing abnormal is found. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.

Investigations reveal marginally elevated liver enzymes, with other routine blood test results within normal limits. An ultrasound examination shows a normal gallbladder with no stones and an incidental finding of a 6.3 cm diameter infra-renal aortic aneurysm. He proceeds to have a computed tomography angiogram that confirms the ultrasound findings of a large infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm measuring 6.3 cm in maximal anteroposterior diameter without any concerning radiological features.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Repeat surveillance ultrasound examination in twelve months
Repeat ultrasound imaging in six months
Referral to a vascular surgery outpatient clinic
attributes.explanation:
The underlying cause of this man's acute abdominal pain is uncertain. It is reasonable to consider a gallstone-related problem, and biliary colic could explain his symptoms. However, the ultrasound findings introduce a significant consideration. The patient has a large, threshold-size infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) measuring 6.3 cm in maximal anteroposterior diameter. He has presented with new, vague abdominal pain with no other obvious cause, and this could be related to his aneurysm.

Current guidelines support treatment of AAAs >5.5 cm in male patients. This size threshold balances the risks of intervention against the annual risk of rupture. The risk of rupture for an AAA measuring 6.0-6.9 cm is approximately 10-20% per year. This aneurysm has reached the threshold size for intervention. Therefore, the patient needs a prompt vascular surgery assessment and consideration of active intervention.

A large AAA, even if asymptomatic, carries a significant risk of rupture. In the event of rupture, mortality is high, exceeding 50% even with prompt treatment. Therefore, delaying active intervention by referring to a vascular surgery outpatient clinic or arranging for lifestyle management would be inappropriate. Immediate vascular surgery review is indicated.

This patient's aneurysm is larger than the recommended surveillance thresholds. Although there is variation in the international guidelines for the surveillance of asymptomatic abdominal aortic aneurysms (AAAs), the 2024 European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) guidelines are most widely followed, including in Australia and New Zealand. The ESVS guidelines recommend the following monitoring schedule for asymptomatic patients according to AAA diameter:

  • 25 - 29 mm: ultrasound every five years 

  • 30 - 39 mm: ultrasound every three years

  • 40 - 49 mm (men) or 40 – 44 mm (women): annual ultrasound 

  • ≥50 mm (men) or ≥45 mm (women): ultrasound every six months.
learningPoints: Abdominal aortic aneurysms (AAAs) greater than 5.5 cm in men generally warrant surgical repair.
The risk of rupture for an Abdominal aortic aneurysms (AAAs) measuring 6.0-6.9 cm is approximately 10-20% per year.
Prompt vascular surgery assessment is indicated for large Abdominal aortic aneurysms (AAAs), especially if symptomatic.
references: ESVS 2024 Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Management of Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm. In: European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) [online]. Accessed 2025 Jun 25. Available at: https://esvs.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/ESVS-2024-AAA-Guidelines.pdf
Cronenwett JL, Johnston KW. Rutherford's vascular surgery E-book. Elsevier Health Sciences. 2014.
Fitridge R, Thompson M. Mechanisms of vascular disease: A reference book for vascular specialists. Adelaide: University of Adelaide Press; 2011.
